nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich areas of lagoon and seaward reefs, at depths of 2–23 m; often found among seagrass beds and rubble zones

diet.notes: Has been documented consuming microalgae (spirulina), small crustaceans (mysis, brine shrimp, krill), zooplankton, and pelagic tunicates. In aquaria, generally advised to offer a variety of meaty frozen foods and vegetable-based preparations such as spirulina flakes.

summary: The Blackmouth Bicolor Chromis (Neoglyphidodon nigroris) is a damselfish known for its vivid yellow-and-black juvenile coloration, though adults tend to fade to brown and black. Generally considered reef-safe, adults tend to become highly aggressive toward tankmates as they mature.
careNotes:
Juvenile Blackmouth Bicolor Chromis display bright yellow coloration with two horizontal black bars, which makes young specimens particularly appealing for reef displays. This coloration typically fades to brown and black as the fish mature. Adults are widely considered to be highly territorial and can become extremely aggressive toward tankmates, which can make selecting compatible species challenging. The species is generally regarded as reef-safe and has not been widely reported to nip at corals or bother invertebrates.

A minimum tank of around 163 liters is commonly recommended, with larger volumes advisable given the species' territorial nature at adult size. Adults reach up to 13 cm in total length, with most aquarium specimens typically staying in the 8–10 cm range.

This species tends to be manageable when juvenile but often becomes significantly more aggressive as it approaches adult size. It is generally best kept with larger, semi-aggressive tankmates capable of holding their own, and is not well suited for community tanks with small or peaceful fish.

Breeding in captivity has been documented; eggs are laid on a hard substrate and guarded by the male. The species is oviparous with distinct pair bonding during spawning.
breedingNotes: The Blackmouth Bicolor Chromis is oviparous, forming distinct pair bonds during breeding. Eggs are attached to a hard substrate surface and are guarded and aerated by the male until hatching. Breeding in home aquaria has been reported but is considered uncommon.

additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, tank size, temperament, diet, and reef-safe status. Lists species under synonym Paraglyphidodon nigroris; listed care level as Easy and temperament as Semi-Aggressive. Tank minimum listed as 20 gallons.

additional.notes: Used for taxonomic confirmation, native range, maximum size (13.0 cm TL), temperature range (25.2–29.3°C), and habitat description. FishBase confirms Neoglyphidodon nigroris as accepted name.
